data.title: TIM S.A. Q4: Solid Guidance Opens Doors For Further Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Brazil's third largest telecom company, TIM S.A. (NYSE:TIMB)—a subsidiary of TIM Italia (OTCPK:TIIAY)—reported solid Q4 2024 earnings results on February 11th, with its ADR jumping double digits in subsequent trading sessions.

The investment thesis for TIM S.A. remains centered on its defensive appeal and strong shareholder returns. The stock continues to be a good dividend payer and relatively less volatile than the market, with a beta of 0.18 over the last twelve months.

Even so, amid an economic crisis in Brazil, where inflation is off target, interest rates in double digits and projected to rise close to high-teens by the end of this year, TIMB's price action has being under pressure over the last twelve months—also driven by a massive devaluation of the BRL versus the U.S. dollar.

In Q4, TIMB's positive price reaction was driven not only by strong net profit growth but also by solid guidance for 2025-2027. The company is projecting sustainable revenue growth, disciplined CapEx spending, and attractive returns for shareholders. That said, I remain bullish on TIMB, and I'm updating my valuation model on TIMB's, where it should reflect a ~10% upside potential.

A Rapid Recap on TIMB

In November last year, I wrote:

“I'm a buyer of TIM S.A. below $15 levels, considering a dividend yield of over 8% as the company plans to distribute $3.5 billion between dividends and interest on capital. The FX can be a major headwind to be observed here for the near term, but the company's valuation levels for such a robust income-paying stock seem too de-risked to ignore for now.”

At the time, I took the right call as TIMB shares were trading at $13.83 per share and have since risen about 10% to $15.21 at the time of writing. As TIMB ended 2024 with a dividend yield of 8.64%, I arrived at the $15 per share price target for the stock, assuming that dividends will grow at a constant rate indefinitely based on a 3% growth rate derived from the industry's 10-year dividend CAGR and TIM S.A. historic EPS growth.

But now that the price target has been hit, the question is whether TIMB needs a re-rating or if we should raise the price target due to the recent results and the strong guidance for the next two years.

TIM S.A. Q4 Results and 2025-2027 Guidance

First, let's revisit what happened in TIM S.A.'s Q4.

To begin with, TIMB missed EPS estimates by 2 cents, reporting $0.37, but beat revenue estimates by $23.9 million, reporting a total of $1.15 billion, or R$6.6 billion, a growth of 5.7% year-over-year.

On the top line, there were no major surprises, following the trend seen in recent quarters, with postpaid as the main driver of growth (9.5% YoY) in the Mobile Service segment (responsible for more than 90% of the company's revenues), while fixed services remained practically stable due to the company's reduced focus on this segment.

Now, on the bottom line side, TIMB missed estimates mainly considering an EBITDA margin of 50.5%, just 0.3 p.p above the same period last year. The company reported an EBITDA of R$3.3 billion, up 6.2% YoY mainly due to the higher than expected performance of mobile devices revenues, which have a lower margin as the company subsidizes these products to build customer loyalty. In any case, net profit came in solid at R$1.1 billion, representing growth of 17.2% YoY, though 6.5% below the market consensus.

But even with a share of disappointment in margins in Q4, what really lifted bullish sentiment post-earnings was the solid guidance provided for 2025 to 2027.

TIM S.A. projects service revenue growth of ~5% in 2025 and a CAGR of 5% until 2027, maintaining stability. EBITDA remains robust, with a projected increase of 6%-8% per year, and discipline in Capex, maintained at R$4.4-4.6 billion per year. Operating cash flow has a strong growth outlook, with an estimated 14%-16% increase in 2025 and a CAGR of 11%-14% by 2027, reflecting greater operating efficiency. In addition, the projection for shareholder remuneration has been raised to R$13.5-14 billion, reinforcing TIM's commitment to sustainable value creation.

TIM S.A.'s Dividends and Valuations

Considering the short-term guidance (2025), where TIM S.A. plans to distribute between R$3.9 billion and R$4.1 billion, this is a considerable increase versus the R$3.5 billion distributed over 2024.

Looking at the estimates for 2025, with TIM S.A. forecast to post a $1.35 EPS in 2025, implying a 99% payout, the yield drops to ~8.9% in 2025 versus a forecast expectation of ~9.5% for this year. However, this decline is due to the share price increasing by roughly 10% since my last analysis.

Using the Gordon Growth Model and updating the consensus post-Q4 with TIM S.A. delivering an EPS of $1.36 (21% YoY growth), maintaining the same payout, and considering a cost of equity of 11.24% (five-year beta of 0.5; 7.4% risk free rate and 7.67% equity risk premium), and an assuming a dividend and earnings per share growth rate of 3%—which could be adequate for a mature company such as TIM S.A.—we have a price target of $16.83 for TIMB's ADR; that is, an upside potential of still 10.65% even after the 13% rise in the stock over the last five trading days.

The Risks

The main risks that could plague TIM S.A.'s bullish thesis are, in my view, in the following order:

(1) Inflation in Brazil, which currently stands at 4.56%, is above the Brazilian Central Bank's target of 3%. As a result, interest rates have been rising throughout 2024, currently standing at 13.25%, with experts forecasting them to reach levels above 15% throughout this year. However, it's worth noting that the guidance provided for 2025 to 2027 for revenue growth of between 5% and 6% outpaces inflation. This is fundamental for the smooth running of TIM S.A.'s business.

(2) Risks related to competitive dynamics are also considerable considering the scenario marked by Telefónica Brasil (VIV) and Claro (AMX), being the top two telecom companies in Brazil, and the NuCel with Nu Holding's (NU) plans to enter in the telecom industry likely intensifying pricing wars and requiring TIM S.A. to have a pricing strategy mainly with a high focus on post-paid, where TIM S.A. generally offers the cheapest plans versus its peers.

(3) Currency fluctuations and deterioration of Brazil's equity premium risk, where such factors can directly influence the distribution of dividends (considering that TIMB pays in BRL and distributes via ADR converting into USD) and a higher premium considerably alters the cost of equity, forcing investors to re-evaluate valuation models.

Final Remarks

TIM S.A. reported a solid and unsurprising Q4, although the bottom line was slightly disappointing, mainly due to issues with the services mix. Most importantly, however, was the robust guidance for 2025 and 2027, reinforcing appealing dividend distributions potentially yielding around 8-9% over the next two years, which are still attractive given Brazil's equity risk premium of 7.6%.

Revisiting the valuation model, with TIMB able to distribute $1.35 in annual dividends, I updated my share price target to $16.83 for the TIM S.A. ADR.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: American Century Focused Dynamic Growth Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio Review

Stocks posted gains. The reelection of former President Donald Trump initially pleased investors who had worried about another protracted legal battle regarding the election's results. However, the Federal Reserve's (Fed's) signal that it will slow the pace of interest rate cuts amid persistent inflation largely erased postelection gains.

Growth outperformed at every capitalization range. Mid- and large-cap growth stocks led U.S. equities higher during the quarter, outperforming small-cap growth stocks, which posted more modest gains. Value stocks declined across the market-capitalization spectrum.

Consumer discretionary was a top contributor. Stock choices drove sector outperformance. Tesla and Amazon were significant contributors.

Industrials benefited performance. Stock selection in the sector was positive. Rocket Labs USA was a top contributor.

Health care detracted. Our overweight allocation relative to the benchmark hampered performance, especially in biotechnology. Regeneron Pharmaceuticals and Alnylam Pharmaceuticals were significant detractors.

Key Contributors

Tesla (TSLA). The electric vehicle maker's recent quarterly earnings were received well by the market, and there has been some excitement about autonomous driving and its new robotaxis. The stock also got an election boost as investors appeared to think a lighter regulatory environment would favor Tesla.

Rocket Lab USA (RKLB). The commercial space exploration company reported better-than- expected revenues and said it has a multiyear backlog of orders. It also won government contracts and CHIPS and Science Act funding and inked its first customers for its forthcoming Neutron rocket.

Microsoft (MSFT). The software giant reported better-than-expected earnings, but the stock fell on investor concerns about Microsoft's spending on artificial intelligence. We own Microsoft but hold a less-than-benchmark position, which benefited relative performance.

Key Detractors

Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN). Although the biotechnology company reported revenue and earnings above expectations, investors appeared to be concerned about disappointing sales of its higher-dose Eylea, which does not face the biosimilar competition that its original Eylea does. Eylea treats age-related macular degeneration.

Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR). The maker of semiconductor-based power solutions for a variety of markets reported earnings that beat analysts' estimates but offered disappointing guidance due to a delay in getting chips.

Broadcom (AVGO). Not owning this semiconductor manufacturer hampered performance. Broadcom has benefited from the market's optimism about artificial intelligence, which is boosting demand for chips.

Notable Trades

ON Semiconductor (ON). We believe computer chipmaker ON Semiconductor is compelling after management has transformed the company into a more profitable, low-capital-intensity, faster-growing semiconductor business.

FANUC (OTCPK:FANUY). We eliminated our stake in FANUC, a factory automation/robot manufacturer. We continue to be strong believers in the secular trend toward factory automation. Nevertheless, we think the company is likely to face headwinds because of its exposure to China. We redeployed the capital into stocks with greater upside and certainty.

Portfolio Positioning

Our process uses bottom-up financial analysis aimed at identifying large-cap companies that we think have the potential to produce attractive long-term earnings growth. We seek to reduce unintended, nonfinancial risks and align the portfolio with company-specific risks that we believe will be rewarded over time. As a result of this approach, our sector and industry allocations reflect areas of the market where we believe we are finding opportunities at a given time.

Focus on enduring growth amid near-term uncertainty. We think market volatility will likely remain high as investors contemplate U.S. policy uncertainty alongside geopolitical risks. While consensus 2025 corporate earnings forecasts have predicted solid growth, we believe valuations on large caps aren't cheap. However, lower interest rates typically do cut financing costs and benefit growth stocks in the long term. As a result, we remain positive on growth stocks because we continue to identify companies that we believe are capable of producing strong, sustainable earnings growth driven by powerful secular trends. Examples include digital advertising and business transformation; artificial intelligence, mobile and cloud computing; process automation; and electric vehicle adoption.

Today's returns depend on tomorrow's earnings. Corporate earnings are key because stock returns are often a function of earnings growth, dividend yield and the price investors are willing to pay for these earnings. The market is typically forward-looking, trading on expectations of future growth. For this reason, we expect investors will focus on full-year 2025 earnings estimates, which still look too high, in our opinion. Overall, we remain generally positive on growth stocks going forward, given falling interest rates and the increasing likelihood of an economic soft landing. Nevertheless, we believe extremely high 2025 earnings forecasts are a potential risk, particularly for companies that fail to meet those lofty expectations.

Productivity is central to profit growth. We see lasting challenges to productivity growth in the movement toward nationalism, deglobalization and demographic trends of social inequality and aging global populations. In general, as worker productivity is critical to corporate profit growth, we remain hopeful that AI and other technologies could help offset these productivity declines over time. Moreover, our research has indicated uncertainty remains high on several fronts, which we think helps explain today's extreme market concentration and recent bouts of volatility. In addition to the economy and interest rates, wars and policy uncertainty introduce other sources of potential volatility, in our view. We believe progress on any or all of these fronts could lead to broader market participation.

Volatility presents opportunities, in our view. Business conditions typically vary from quarter to quarter and year to year, and stocks go up or down in the near term for any number of reasons. However, we believe companies with solid long-term growth prospects are better situated to ride out uncertainty relating to economic and earnings growth. As a result, we believe our portfolio investments have significant long-term growth opportunities. As ever, we remain vigilant in monitoring what we view as the financial progress and risks of our investments. We seek to utilize short-term volatility as an opportunity to add to positions when we see share prices disconnect from our assessment of their long-term valuation potential.

We believe individual security selection is preferable to binary market calls. While we think there's a tendency to think in binary terms about electoral outcomes, Fed interest rate changes and economic data points, we would also argue that individual companies respond differently to macroeconomic conditions. Indeed, we believe we've found opportunities in diverse companies developing new products and technologies that are transforming entire sectors and industries. We don't view these as top-down solutions. Rather, we rely on our bottom-up financial research to identify individual companies that we think are innovating and reimagining their competitive landscapes. That's why we seek to invest in dynamic, innovative growth companies with healthy balance sheets and cash flows that we believe can improve throughout the economic cycle.

Goal and Strategy

Long-term capital appreciation through investments in 30-45 quality, large-cap, U.S. growth oriented companies.

However, when doing so, the portfolio managers may not consider ESG factors with respect to every investment decision and, even when such factors are considered, they may conclude that other attributes of an investment outweigh ESG considerations when making decisions for the portfolio. The consideration of ESG factors may limit the investment opportunities available to a portfolio, and the portfolio may perform differently than those that do not incorporate ESG considerations. ESG data used by the portfolio managers often lacks standardization, consistency, and transparency, and for certain companies such data may not be available, complete, or accurate.

data.title: LyondellBasell: Cyclical Bargain With A 7% Yield
data.description:
Cyclical stocks can make great wealth compounders if purchased at a low price and held for the income that they generate. Such was the case in 2020 when oil giants like Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX) traded at multi-year lows, giving patient, long-term investors an opportunity to accumulate before oil prices eventually turned around.

Such appears to be the case right now with LyondellBasell (NYSE:LYB), which I last covered in November 2024, highlighting its near-term challenges, strategic focus on circular solutions, value-enhancing program, and attractive valuation. The stock has continued to see weakness since my last piece on the prolonged market slump, giving a -10% return since my last piece while the S&P 500 (SPY) rose by 2.8%.

In this article, I revisit LYB including recent business performance and outlook, and discuss why the current low cycle presents a solid buying opportunity for long-term investors, so let’s dive in!

Why LYB?

LyondellBasell is a leading global chemical company that’s one of the largest producers of polymers such as polyethylene, polypropylene, and oxyfuels. While LYB itself isn’t a household name, its proprietary technologies produce feedstocks that power the modern world. Industries served by LYB include healthcare, automotive, construction, food safety, clean water, and transportation.

It's no secret that LYB has seen headwinds over the past year. This includes lower global growth, particularly in China, higher energy costs in Europe, and global capacity that’s outpaced demand growth. These dynamics were reflected in LYB’s Q4 2024 results, in which Americas Olefins & Polyolefins EBITDA declined by 18% YoY to $496 million, due to lower margins that were partially offset by higher polyethylene export volumes.

Meanwhile, Europe, Asia, & International Olefines & Polyolefins saw continued challenges due to higher feedstock costs and Q4 maintenance, which pressured margins and volumes. The $1.16 EBITDA loss in this segment was driven in large part by $1 billion in Europe and Asia asset write-downs (a non-cash item). Despite challenges in the core olefins & polyolefins business, LYB still generated a solid $3.8 billion in operating cash flow during all of 2024, which provided 2x coverage on the $1.9 billion in dividends and share buybacks during all of last year.

Looking ahead, management expects to see a rebound in demand as customers begin restocking after inventory drawdowns last year. They also believe that the current downturn won’t last forever and that global demand for durable goods will inevitably return following this post-pandemic downturn and that the company is positioned to surpass historical performance. As shown below, LYB’s upside potential to average historical margins is significant, with current margins sitting at 60% of the historical average.

Management also plans to reduce costs through a value enhancement program, which is expected to yield $1 billion in recurring annual EBITDA savings by the end of this year. Management is also positioning the company to exit non-core businesses, including refining, while investing in high-return projects such as MoReTec chemical recycling facilities in Germany and Houston as part of its CLCS (Circular and Low-Carbon Solutions) business.

For CLCS, LYB is targeting growth at $1 billion in incremental EBITDA from 2 million tons of annual volumes by the end of this decade. The CLCS business holds promise for LYB, with 65% volume growth last year to 200K tons across the product lines of mechanical recycling, chemical recycling, and renewable feedstocks. Management highlighted a positive long-term demand outlook for this industry despite near term supply headwinds during the recent earnings call:

Market demand for circular plastic remains robust. Consumer preferences and brand owner commitments to increase utilization of recycled plastics or driving demand growth. And European regulation is bolstering demand by mandating increased utilization of recycled content in plastic packaging. But this transition to circularity takes time.

Demand growth is merely being delayed due to lack of capacity, but not being eliminated. As such, LYB and other industry observers are incorporating these constraints by scaling back 2030 forecasts for industry capacity and the addressable market. The mega trends and investment thesis remain intact. The market for circular plastics is expected to be short of supply and supportive of attractive margins for quite some time.

Meanwhile, LYB maintains a solid balance sheet and a BBB credit rating with stable outlook from S&P. This is supported by a safe net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 1.8x. LYB also currently yields an appealing 7%. The dividend was raised by 7% last year, marking 14 consecutive years of annual increases. As noted on the last earnings call, management remains committed to returning 70% of free cash flow to shareholders.

Lastly, I continue to see value in LYB at the current price of $76.70 with a forward PE of 12.5. EPS is estimated to decline by 4% this year and is already baked into the 12.5x forward PE. However, analysts estimate a sharp rebound next year with 34% growth in EPS followed by another 11% growth in 2027. As such, I believe LYB is sitting close to the bottom, presenting material upside potential in the event of a cyclical recovery.

Risks to the thesis include the potential for geopolitical tensions due to tariffs and/or conflict, which could delay a potential recovery in margins. Macroeconomic pressures due to inflation could also be a headwind for demand recovery and for margins. Plus, increased supply from competitors absent a demand recovery could also be a headwind for LYB.

Investor Takeaway

LyondellBasell presents a compelling long-term investment opportunity as a cyclical stock currently trading at depressed levels. It’s currently undergoing near-term headwinds from lower global growth, elevated energy costs, and supply-demand imbalances. However, LYB remains financially strong with robust cash flow generation, a solid balance sheet, while paying a 7% yield that’s covered by free cash flow.

Management's cost-cutting initiatives, strategic divestitures, and investments in high-growth circular solutions position the company for future profitability as demand rebounds. With its valuation near cyclical lows and earnings projected to rebound significantly in the coming years, LYB offers material upside potential for patient investors willing to navigate short-term volatility.

Lastly, I am upgrading LYB from a 'Buy' to 'Strong Buy' on material share price weakness since I last covered the stock. This creates a high-yielding opportunity that could result in potential market-beating total returns for long-term investors from here.

data.title: VTEC Or NCA: Picking A California Muni Bond Fund
data.description:
Introduction

Most of my articles on municipal bond funds have focused on Closed-End-Funds, or CEFs, as they usually yield more and can provide the possibility of purchasing at a discount. Here I will compare the Nuveen California Municipal Value Fund (NYSE:NCA), a CEF, to the low-cost Vanguard California Tax-Exempt Bond ETF (BATS:VTEC), a new ETF. The fact that NCA is a non-levered fund helps makes this a valid comparison.

When comparing VTEC since it started just over, VTEC would be rated a Buy for investors looking for a smooth ride but it CAGR was inferior to competitors. NCA investors should consider swapping too as its long-term return was lowest of the Nuveen California Municipal Bind CEFs. A set of better choices is provided at the end of this article, including links to my prior articles on similar funds.

Nuveen California Municipal Value Fund review

nca

Seeking Alpha describes this fund as:

The Fund’s primary investment objective is to provide current income exempt from regular federal and California income taxes. The Fund’s secondary investment objective is to enhance portfolio value relative to the California municipal bond market by investing in tax-exempt California municipal securities that the Fund’s investment adviser and/or the Fund’s sub-adviser believes are underrated or undervalued or that represent municipal market sectors that are undervalued. Benchmark: S&P Municipal Bond California TR. The fund started in 1987.

Source: seeking-alpha NCA

Nuveen mentions these investing guidelines for their fund:

The Fund may invest up to 20% of its managed assets in municipal securities rated below investment quality or judged by the manager to be of comparable quality, of which up to 10% of its managed assets may be rated below B-/B3 or of comparable quality. The Fund may invest in inverse floating rate municipal securities, also known as tender option bonds.

Source: nuveen NCA

NCA has $308m in AUM and comes with 54bps in fees, of which the managers get 48bps. Latest yield was 4%.

Holdings review

One feature I like about Nuveen is that provide lots of criterial data about each CEF's portfolio.

Notice that the duration is under 8 years while the effective maturity is almost 19 years. The difference is due to all the interest payments made on the bonds held. For those effected by AMT, over 13% of the interest from NCA counts toward those calculations. At only 137, the number of bonds held is very small. To me, that does make NCA riskier than VTEC which holds almost 2400 issues.

As with other asset classes, there are sectors within the municipal bond market.

Credit quality is very high as I calculated the portfolio as AA- rated.

With only 2% of the portfolio maturing in the next four years, that will help prop up the payouts if rates start declining in California.

Related to that, under 20% of the portfolio is callable over the next two years.

When I looked at the total portfolio, the 137 bonds come from about 60 different issues, with only the CSCDA Community Improvement Authority (16 @ 4.5%) and Los Angeles (13 @ 14.6%). Another 2% are from issuers in the greater Los Angeles area. I found one Seeking Alpha article on how the fires around Los Angles could effect the municipal bond market.

Top 10 issues

The low holdings total shows up in the fact that eight issues all have weights over 2%. VTEC has none over 1%. The average coupon rate stands at 4.13%.

Distributions review

The distribution coverage ratio stands at 99.2%, down slightly from a year ago. The infrequent change in the payouts is due to Nuveen using what is referred to as a "managed distribution policy". The main benefit to investors is a more stable income flow.

Vanguard California Tax-Exempt Bond ETF review

Seeking Alpha describes this fund as:

The investment seeks to track the performance of the S&P California AMT-Free Municipal Bond Index. This index measures the performance of the investment-grade segment of the California municipal bond market as determined by the index provider, S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All of the fund’s investments will be selected through the sampling process, and at least 80% of the fund’s assets will be invested in securities held in the index. Benchmark: S&P California AMT-Free Muni Bond TR USD. The fund started in early 2024.

Source: seeking-alpha.com VTEC

VTEC has $528m in AUM and charges only 8bps in fees; a big advantage over NCA that charges 54 bps. The current yield is 2.8%, giving NCA a 1.2% advantage.

Index review

Unlike the Nuveen fund, which is actively managed, VTEC holds bonds based on an index. Here is a brief overview of the index construction rules.

Holdings review

Vanguard does not provide as many data points for their ETF.

The portfolio has both a shorter maturity and duration compared to NCA. Another important pieces of data Vanguard does not provide is sectors or issuers but at over 2300 issues, that probably is less important than a concentrate fund like NCA. There is both ratings and maturity data though.

Morningstar gives VTEC's portfolio an AA rating, one notch above what I rated NCA at. VTEC has a higher percentage maturing in the near future.

By using ticker data for each bond, I was able to find each issuer with a 2+% weight in the portfolio.

There are ten different Los Angeles issuers with a combined portfolio weight of 20%.

Top holdings

This is a list of every position with a weight of .25% or greater.

Distributions review

Unlike NCA, VTEC does not try to manage the distributions. History is too short to detect any pattern or direction payouts are taking.

Comparing funds

Factor NCA VTEC
AUM $308m $528m
Fees 54bps 8bps
Issues held 137 2360
Yield 4.00% 2.80%
Discount NA
Credit rating AA- AA

So far, VTEC offers both a smoother ride and a better return, though one year of data has limited value to me.

That shows up in the data points provided too.

Basically, investors can choose to give up some yield in return foe less volatility by owning VTEC versus NCA.

Portfolio strategy

Both funds are designed for high income residents in California where the top income bracket has a marginal rate of 12.3%. California also levies a 1% mental health services tax on income exceeding $1 million, making the state's highest tax rate 13.3%! Along with not owing federal taxes, residents avoid state and maybe local taxes too. While both of these funds concentrate on investment-grade bonds, there are funds like the Nuveen California High Yield Municipal Bond Fund (NCHRX) that hold mostly below IG bonds. For investors liking CEFs but with the leverage factor, the Nuveen California Select Tax-Free Income Portfolio (NXC) has a similar yield and duration as NCA.

Another advantage, besides avoiding federal income taxes, is the municipal bond asset class has very low correlation to stocks and some un-correlation to US bonds.

Final thoughts

Here I provide links to other California Municipal Bond funds articles I’ve done. Afterwards is a results table listing each ticker, plus those covered here.

  • MUC Vs. NAC: Comparing California Quality Muni Funds
  • NKX Vs. PCQ: Comparing CA-Only Muni CEFs

It appears there is a clear winner over the past decade (NKX) and clear poor performer (PCQ). I did not use NKX in this article since it has a 41% leverage ratio and I did not that as a fair comparison to an ETF.

If an investor decides to own more than one, pick funds with a low correlation to each other, as some have that characteristic.

data.title: Bid-Ask Spreads Wide Under Canadian Shelter Prices
data.description:
Despite a 200 basis point drop in the Bank of Canada’s overnight rate, Canadian mortgage rates have more than doubled from the pandemic’s easy credit days when buyers were engulfed in bidding wars (5-year fixed rates offered by banks, below since April 2020, courtesy of WOW.ca).

So far, sellers’ high hopes have kept asking prices elevated, even as the supply of new listings (especially in the 1m+ range) is dramatically outpacing sales in most areas. The average price of homes that have sold offers a reality check.

In January, the average home list price in Greater Vancouver was $1,208,415, down 3.6% compared to January 2024, but $193,000 over the average selling price in the past two weeks of $1.15m, down 20.87% year-over-year (the average number of sales in grey and the average sale price in red below since January 2020, courtesy of Property.ca).

The average asking price in Toronto was $1,033,742, a 2.7% decrease from the previous year but still $309k over the average sale price for the past two weeks of $724,000 (-33% year over year).

Surrounding the Greater Toronto Area, cities show similar gaps between average asking and sold prices.

In Mississauga, west of Toronto, the average asking price is $977,833 + 2.8% compared to January 2024, but the average sale price the past two weeks was $673,000, -33.3% year over year.

In Vaughan, just north of Toronto, the average asking price was $1,033,742, down 2.7% from the previous year, while the average sale price was $681,000, down 48% year-over-year.

In Barrie, one hour north, the median list price was $764,109 in January, reflecting a 2.72% decrease from December 2024 but $159,000 above the average sale price in February of $605k, -15% year-over-year.

Currently, most for-sale listings in these major population areas are in the $1m+ asking range. Something has to give.

Affordable home prices compare favourably to rents and household income.

Consider a 600-square-foot, one-bedroom, one-bath, one-parking-space Barrie condo, recently marked down from an asking price of $369k to $325k. The monthly maintenance fees are $554, and the property taxes are $2,071 annually. With a 10% downpayment, the monthly carrying costs amount to $2,583 plus utilities and maintenance (as shown below).

Similar units were selling for closer to $500,000 in the mania of 2021-2022. So anyone who bought back then is underwater. While the current asking price of $325,000 is moving in the right direction, it remains some 4.6x the median Canadian household income, around $70k annually.

Historically, the well-established ‘affordable’ norm is about 3x household income, or a home price of approximately 210K today. So, $325k is still too high.

Those considering current prices should compare monthly carrying costs to rents for similar properties. Where market rents are less than the total monthly carrying costs, buying remains mathematically unattractive for those looking to occupy the property personally or as an investment to rent out to others.

As more properties are listed for rent and sale, the cost of shelter is coming down. That will be a big plus for future buyers and renters as excesses of the recent property bubble reverse. More downside progress is needed, and the spring market is set to be revealing.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Amazon's Layoffs And Robotics Initiatives Are A Boost To Earnings
data.description:
Amazon’s (NASDAQ:AMZN) recent earnings received mixed feedback from analysts. The company was able to beat the estimates but gave a modest forward growth outlook. However, it is important to dig deeper to find the new trends reported by the company. In the recent quarter, the operating income of AWS came at $10.6 billion, which was a staggering 48% YoY growth. The next few quarters could be difficult for AWS as it will face tough comps. This will inevitably reduce the forward YoY operating income growth momentum of AWS. On the other hand, Amazon is showing a very positive trend in terms of reducing shipping costs. In the recent quarter, the worldwide shipping costs grew by 4% YoY compared to 11% YoY growth in worldwide paid units. In the previous article, it was mentioned that the cloud business will face some headwinds as competitors increase their market share.

There has been a gradual improvement in shipping costs over the last few quarters as the company leans heavily on robotics and automation within its warehouses. Prior to the pandemic, the shipping costs were growing at more than 20 percentage points higher than the paid units, which was having a negative impact on overall margins. This has changed completely in the last few quarters. There has been a YoY incremental operating income of $2.8 billion in the North America segment, which is close to the $3.5 billion YoY incremental operating income reported by AWS. The recent headcount reduction is also having a positive impact on Amazon's margins, and we could see continuous improvement as more automation is done within the shipping service.

The higher efficiency within fulfillment could be a long-term trend as the company uses better automation and robotics within warehouses and for last-mile delivery over the next few years. This will have a strong impact on the EPS growth momentum. For the fiscal year ending Dec 2027, Amazon stock is trading at only 24 times the EPS estimate, which is quite modest when we compare with other big tech peers and look at the EPS growth trajectory of the stock.

Looking Beyond AWS

AWS has been the most important segment for Amazon over the last few years. There has been a big improvement in the operating margin of AWS in 2024 which led to margins of 36%-38%. This increased the operating income of AWS and the overall company. AWS was the key reason behind the rapid earnings growth of 2024. However, this could change in the next few quarters as this segment faces tougher comps from the first quarter of 2025.

Figure: AWS metric in the last few quarters. Source: Amazon Filings

In Q1 2024, AWS reported a staggering 83% YoY operating income growth as its operating margin increased to 37.6%. This trend continued for the remaining quarters with a strong margin trend. This will make tougher comps for AWS in the next few quarters. Despite the AI tailwind, it is unlikely that the operating margin of AWS can increase to the 40%-45% range. Both Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOG) are ramping up their own cloud businesses, increasing the competitive pressure on AWS.

Next Phase of Earnings Growth

We could see a more modest growth trend in AWS and advertising over the next few quarters due to tougher comps. The next major earnings growth driver could be its core e-commerce business. Over the last decade, Amazon has been diverting the profits from AWS and advertising to build a more robust fulfillment network. This is finally showing positive results. It should be noted that the retail business runs on wafer-thin margins, unlike AWS. However, even a few percentage point improvement in margins in the e-commerce business can deliver massive operating income growth due to the huge revenue base.

Despite a strong revenue growth in the last few quarters, Amazon continues to bring efficiency within its headcount. We have heard of massive layoffs in various divisions over the last few quarters. These layoffs have helped improve the overall margins for the company. We could see a faster pace of efficiency as more powerful robots are installed in more warehouses.

Figure: Employee headcount trend of last few quarters. Source: Amazon Filings

In the recent quarter, the worldwide shipping costs reported 4% YoY growth compared to 11% YoY growth in worldwide paid units. This is a 7 percentage point gap. In the year-ago quarter, the worldwide shipping costs showed 11% YoY growth compared to 12% YoY paid units growth. Hence, we can see a clear improvement in the fulfillment costs for Amazon.

Figure: Shipping costs for Amazon in the last few quarters. Source: Amazon Filings

The importance of this trend becomes more stark when we compare it to pre-pandemic numbers. In Q4 2019, Amazon reported 43% YoY shipping costs growth and only 22% YoY worldwide paid units growth. Hence, the gap was negative 21 percentage points.

Figure: Shipping costs prior to the pandemic. Source: Amazon Filings

The trend for most of the quarters in 2019 was negative, where Amazon showed massive growth in shipping costs compared to the growth in paid units delivered. This had a negative impact on earnings. AWS and advertising business supported the e-commerce business during this time.

Figure: Improvement in operating income of North America segment. Source: Amazon Filings

The improvement in fulfillment costs is already having a big impact. In the recent quarter, Amazon reported $9.2 billion in operating income in the North America segment, which was 43% YoY growth. In the year-ago quarter, this segment reported $6.4 billion in operating income.

Figure: Improvement in the International segment. Source: Amazon Filings

The $2.8 billion in YoY incremental operating income in North America segment is close to the $3.5 billion in YoY incremental operating income reported by AWS. Similarly, the international segment has shown $1.3 billion in operating income, compared to negative $0.4 billion in the year-ago quarter. This is a $1.7 billion improvement in this segment. Some of this improvement is due to the higher advertising revenue on the Amazon platform. However, a big chunk has been due to greater efficiency in fulfillment.

Robotics and Automation Will Take Over

Amazon has been investing heavily in robotics and automation for the last few years. We have started seeing the company increase the use of robots within the warehouses to simplify tasks. An Amazon executive has mentioned that close to 70% of shipping was automated within two years of building these robots. Robots like Cardinal and Proteus are particularly helpful to the company to automate repetitive tasks and reduce worker injury.

Another major cost for Amazon is last-mile delivery. The company is looking to automate this through its robotaxi platform Zoox. The robotaxi platform is already ready for commercialization, and we could see it compete with Waymo over the next few quarters. However, the bigger cost saving would be using Zoox as a last-mile delivery platform, which could allow massive improvement in margins. Autonomous last-mile delivery is also likely to be a lower-risk service because it does not require transporting people, and it can drive at a significantly slower speed.

As mentioned above, we could continue to see improvement in the gap between shipping costs and paid units over the next few quarters and years as more robots get online. This would be a strong tailwind for Amazon’s operating income expansion and would also give the company a better moat within the e-commerce industry.

Future Trajectory of the Stock

For a long time, Amazon stock was trading at very high multiples due to low margins of the company. This is changing rapidly as Amazon delivers strong operating income growth. Amazon stock is now trading at a forward PE multiple which is quite modest and even lower than many other big tech peers. The EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 is $7.57 giving the stock a forward PE multiple of 30.2. The EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2027 is $9.48 giving the stock a forward PE multiple of only 24.1.

Figure: Forward EPS estimates of Amazon. Source: Seeking Alpha

If the current improvement in fulfillment costs continues, we could see an upward revision in Amazon’s margins and EPS in the next few quarters. This would be one of the long-term trends helping Amazon stock.

Apple has a higher forward PE multiple of 26.9 when we look at EPS estimates for 2 fiscal years ahead. It should be noted that Apple's forward EPS growth projections are also very modest, giving it a higher PEG ratio than Amazon. Over the last three years, Apple's revenue has been largely stagnant and most of the EPS growth has been due to buybacks. On the other hand, Amazon has shown rapid improvement in margins. If the forward shipping costs continue to decline, we should see further operating income and EPS growth by Amazon.

The slowdown in revenue growth for Amazon is cited as a risk by some analysts. However, Amazon is still expected to deliver close to double-digit growth in revenue over the next few years. Expansion of margins should help the company deliver a bigger EPS growth trajectory. Robotics and automation give the company a better moat than other rivals, making it a better bet in the big tech space.

Investor Takeaway

Amazon is showing strong improvement in margins within fulfillment by using robotics and automation. This is a long-term trend that should help in the expansion of overall margins. Even a few percentage point improvement in fulfillment costs can have a massive impact on the bottom line due to the higher revenue base of the e-commerce business.

Amazon stock is trading at only 24 times the EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2027. This is quite modest when we look at the EPS growth momentum and compare it with other big tech peers. Amazon has a stronger moat, which is improving as the company increases investment in robotics and automation.

data.title: Medtronic plc 2025 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Medtronic plc in conjunction with their 2025 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: The Power Of Patience - 3 Of My Favorite Dividend Growth Stocks
data.description:
Introduction

On February 14, I wrote an article covering my entire portfolio, which you can access here if you like. So far, it has close to 300 comments, which is one of the highest numbers I have encountered in a very long time.

So, to everyone who participated, thank you!

As I have said in so many articles, the comment section is a great source of information for a wide range of people, including me. For example, many readers brought up fantastic questions, highlighting my lack of technology exposure and the fact that I do not own many stocks with high, double-digit dividend growth.

These comments are entirely justified. As I am 29 and have no plans of retiring anytime soon, I technically have decades of dividend growth ahead of me - God willing, of course.

With that in mind, one of the reasons why I have increasingly focused on higher-yielding stocks in recent months is because I believe we will see a rotation from growth to value stocks.

This is what I wrote in an article titled "Buckle Up Buttercup: My Warning To Dividend Investors" (emphasis added):

I have been concerned about lofty market valuations, persistent above-average inflation, and geopolitical risks, and the Fed's recent actions have, unfortunately, reinforced my concerns.

The market's negative reaction to the rate cut, especially the upward revision of future rate expectations, signals a growing acceptance of "higher for longer."

While this situation presents challenges, I believe it also creates opportunities.

My focus is shifting towards high-quality cyclical dividend growth stocks, which offer compelling valuations, healthy balance sheets, pricing power(!!!) and attractive yields, despite the current economic uncertainty.

On top of that, I decided to spend more time on income opportunities because many of my readers are retired.

Having said all of this, in this article, I'll focus on the "growth" aspect of dividend growth. Three stocks with low yields, fantastic business models, and a high probability of elevated long-term (dividend) growth.

I own two of them and will likely add the third one this year, as I believe I need to add technology exposure.

So, without further ado, let's get to it!

Reasons To Buy Dividend Growth

More often than not, when I cover low-yielding dividend stocks, some people get very angry with me. They say that I shouldn't call a dividend stock with a low yield a dividend stock because one needs to buy a lot of it in order to generate a decent income.

That is correct - at least the income part.

After all, if we invest $10,000 in a dividend stock with a 0.4% yield, we get $40 in annual dividends. That won't even get you groceries for a week.

So, regardless of what I'm going to say next, investors who need to squeeze the most income out of every dollar invested won't like any of the picks I present.

However, everyone with a long-term horizon and some capital for dividend growth stocks may benefit, as dividend growth and high-yield strategies both have merits.

In 4Q24, ProShares wrote an article about how dividend growth stocks can add a more balanced approach, as it noted the following:

The immediate gratification of high-yield income may be tempting. But it is only one component of total return—the larger part being capital appreciation. It is capital appreciation that has delivered most of the market’s returns over time. - ProShares

As we can see below, since at least 2005, consistent dividend growers have "crushed" the Dow Jones U.S. Select Dividend Index.

Even better, regardless of the interest rate environment, dividend growth has been the place to be, creating an "all-weather" approach for investors.

With that said, there's a reason I brought up the need for a longer-term time horizon. After all, this strategy requires patience when the goal is to generate income.

As we can see below, the average S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrat (used as a benchmark for dividend growth stocks) had an annual dividend growth rate of 7.8%. This is roughly 300 basis points above the average high-yield growth rate.

Over time, this causes dividend growth stocks to offer a higher yield on cost than high-yield stocks, making these stocks superior picks for both income and growth. But then again, you'll need some patience to benefit from both of these things.

Based on that context, dividend growth isn't a topic we neglect.

Now, let's dive into the picks, which include one stock to finally add technology exposure to my portfolio.

Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) - A FANG Stock I Like

Alphabet is a company I have discussed in a number of comments, as quite a few readers brought it up. I also highlighted it in a few multi-ticker articles in recent months, as I have become a fan of the company.

For starters, the company behind the Google brand yields just 0.4% after declaring its first-ever dividend on April 25, 2024. Back then, it also announced an additional buyback boost of $70 billion, which is more than the entire market cap of the smallest 350 S&P 500 members(!).

I believe Alphabet is one of the best growth stocks on the market, as it continues to show the world it's capable of elevated growth in high-demand areas.

For example, in 4Q24, the company generated $96 billion in revenues, 12% more compared to the prior-year quarter. This was supported by an operating margin of 32% (up from 27%) and growth across the board - even in its Google Search segment, which many feared would suffer from AI competition.

Even better, the company is using AI to its advantage, with Gemini 2.0 models being integrated across its products. While AI competition is fierce, the company has a HUGE installed base, as more than 90% of internet users use Google Search. YouTube has more than 2 billion monthly users, and its Android operating system is used by more than 3 billion people.

In order to fuel growth, the company is expected to invest roughly $75 billion in CapEx this year, using AI to enhance its existing services. I added emphasis to the quote below:

Third, our Products and Platforms put AI into the hands of billions of people around the world. We have seven Products and Platforms with over 2 billion users and all are using Gemini. That includes Search, where Gemini is powering our AI overviews. People use Search more with AI overviews and usage growth increases over time as people learn that they can ask new types of questions. This behavior is even more pronounced with younger users who really appreciate the speed and efficiency of this new format. - GOOGL 4Q24 Earnings Call

With that said, the valuation is fascinating, as Alphabet trades at just 22.7x earnings. This is despite high growth expectations, as analysts expect 11% EPS growth in 2025 to be followed by 14% and 15% growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively. This is according to the FactSet data in the chart below.

Using its 10-year average P/E ratio of 24.9x, we get a total return outlook of 17% per year.

I believe Alphabet is so "cheap" because the market isn't convinced AI isn't a Google killer. If Alphabet fails to innovate, that could indeed be the case.

However, I am willing to bet on the opposite, which could lead to substantial returns and elevated dividend growth. After all, it has a payout ratio of a mere 10% and a fantastic growth outlook, even in an environment of elevated uncertainty.

Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL) - A High-Conviction Investment Of Mine

Last year, one of the stocks I bought was Old Dominion Freight Line, a company I had on my radar for many years.

Although it yields just 0.5%, it has a payout ratio of less than 20% and a five-year dividend CAGR of 36%. Moreover, since announcing a dividend in 2017, it has bought back 14% of its shares. While that may not seem like a lot, ODFL is the only company in its industry buying back stock.

Even better, ODFL is the only company with a positive net cash balance, meaning it has more cash than gross debt. As we can see below, the company has $109 million in cash and a mere $40 million in net debt, down from $60 million at the end of 2023.

The engine behind these fantastic numbers is its less-than-truckload ("LTL") business model. Unlike truckload operators, the company moves shorter distances of roughly 915 miles per trip - on average.

LTL is so powerful because it relies on building a superior network of service centers that connect shippers to their customers. ODFL has done a terrific job, as it has built a network of more than 260 service centers in 48 states. Most of these are owned by the company.

Because of its efficient investments, it has a superior service, which provides the company with satisfied customers and a lot of pricing power.

As a result, this network has allowed it to grow its revenue by 12% per year since 2002, almost entirely organic, meaning no major M&A was involved to achieve this.

This has boosted its market share from 2.9% in 2002 to 12.0% in 2023.

Unfortunately, right now, the company is suffering from cyclical weakness. In 4Q24, revenue declined by 7.3% due to lower volumes. However, it still achieved a 99% on-time service ratio and a cargo claims ratio of less than 0.1%.

It also maintained an operating ratio of slightly less than 76.0%, which is a stunning number, as it means that just 76% of its revenues were used to operate the business (operating expenses).

While that may seem like a lot, it's a mind-blowing number in an industry where most operate with operating ratios above 90%. In the truckload business, operating ratios close to 100% are not uncommon.

Because of these issues, dividend growth in February was "just" 7.7%.

Going forward, I'm a lot more upbeat, as I expect a cyclical growth bottom in the United States, as I wrote in a recent article. That's also why I expect dividend growth to rise substantially in the years ahead, as LTL is a big part of the domestic industrial economy and a major winner of economic re-shoring.

Because of a poor performance in 2024 and high expectations, ODFL is the only stock of the three in this article that's not cheap.

Currently, ODFL trades at 37.5x earnings. The good news is that even in this environment, analysts expect 19% and 12% EPS growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively, growth rates that blow most cyclical stocks out of the water.

Personally, I expect actual numbers to come in higher, as a potential cyclical growth bottom would fuel pricing power and total volumes. In 2021 and 2022, this led to 57% and 37% EPS growth, respectively.

While ODFL is the wrong choice for income-focused investors (like all stocks in this article), I am eagerly buying more stock during corrections, as it's one of my highest-conviction ideas.

Carlisle Companies (CSL) - We Just Bought A Lot More

Just like ODFL, Carlisle is a high-conviction dividend growth investment I just bought for my parents as well. In fact, due to the recent stock price decline, we doubled that position, as the CSL ticker is one of our favorite investments.

That's also why I have brought it up in a number of articles this year.

Our bullishness is based on a number of reasons, including its fantastic position in commercial and residential construction. These markets benefit from strong secular growth tailwinds, as the average commercial building is 55 years old, and residential construction has suffered from consistent supply shortages since the Great Financial Crisis.

Carlisle generates roughly 70% of its revenue from construction materials. The remaining 30% are generated through weatherproofing technologies. Slightly more than 60% of all sales are used for replacement and remodeling operations - mainly targeting commercial buyers in the United States.

Moreover, as we can see above, it has superior financials, as its return on invested capital is roughly 25%. This means the company puts capital to work in a very efficient way, which has been a big factor in growing its business.

By 2030, the company aims to generate at least $40 in adjusted earnings per share, supported by 5% average annual organic revenue growth and adjusted EBITDA margins of no less than 25%.

Additionally, the company has a track record of resilience, as we can see below. Even during the Great Financial Crisis and the 2020/2021 pandemic, it maintained solid revenues and was able to grow/maintain its favorable margin profile.

This has also benefited its dividend.

Although CSL yields just 1.1%, it has a subdued payout ratio of just 18.3% and a five-year CAGR of 15.5%. Last year, it hiked its dividend by roughly 18%.

Even better, the dividend has been hiked for 47 consecutive years, making it one of the fastest-growing soon-to-be Dividend Kings on the market.

All of this also explains why Carlisle has consistently beaten the S&P 500, as we can see below.

Currently, the stock price has entered some turmoil, as investors are pricing in cyclical headwinds:

These headwinds included higher interest rates, restricting lending conditions and unfavorable weather patterns. These factors negatively impacted sales and the results were below our midyear 2024 outlook. - CSL 4Q24 Earnings Call

The good news is that Carlisle sticks to its Vision 2030. I would even make the case that if the company were to abandon its target, it would be priced in, as CSL stock trades at a highly favorable valuation.

Currently, CSL trades at just 17.5x earnings, which is well below its ten-year average of 20.5x and comes with a favorable growth outlook, as analysts expect 11-13% average annual EPS growth through at least 2027.

If we incorporate a P/E normalization, we get a path to a $580 stock price, roughly 60% above the current price.

While none of these picks are suitable for investors in desperate need of income, I believe they all have what it takes to beat the market over time, supported by fantastic business models, a focus on shareholder value, and secular growth tailwinds.

Takeaway

In this article, I revisited my portfolio strategy and emphasized the importance of balancing high-yield and dividend-growth stocks.

While I've leaned toward higher-yielding stocks recently, I also recognize the value of low-yield, high-growth opportunities for long-term investors.

Stocks like Alphabet, Old Dominion Freight Line, and Carlisle Companies are perfect examples of this approach, as they offer strong business models, pricing power, and significant dividend growth potential.

Though these picks may not suit income-focused investors, they align with my belief in the power of patience and compounding.

Needless to say, as I continue to adapt my portfolio, I remain committed to finding opportunities that balance income, growth, and resilience in an uncertain market.

Risks To My Thesis

While I'm confident in my focus on dividend growth stocks, there are risks to consider.

A prolonged economic downturn could pressure cyclical stocks like Old Dominion and Carlisle, delaying growth. Additionally, Alphabet's heavy reliance on AI innovation leaves it vulnerable to competition and technological shifts.

Higher-for-longer interest rates could also weigh on valuations and consumer demand, impacting all three companies.

Lastly, dividend growth strategies require patience, as they simply do not have the payout that higher-yielding stocks have.

data.title: S&P 500 Rises As News Headlines Miss The Week's Biggest Story
data.description:
The market-moving headlines in the week were pretty bad for Wall Street bulls. A higher-than-expected consumer inflation report on Wednesday, 12 February 2025 would all but seem to have diminished the chances of even one rate cut during 2025. At least, that's the news the market-moving headlines proclaimed.

But by the end of the trading week, investor expectations for the outlook for interest rate cuts in 2025 changed to go in a different direction. The CME Group's FedWatch Tool closed out the week anticipating a quarter point rate cut to be announced after the Fed meets on 7 May (2025-Q2), about 12 weeks earlier than it forecast a week earlier. While that remains the only rate change expected in 2025, the FedWatch tool suggests another quarter point rate cut is likely in January 2026, which could move up into 2025-Q4 if the momentum for the change in expectations continues.

That change coincided with investors shifting their attention once again to 2025-Q4, which coincides with a 1.5% increase in the level of the S&P 500 over the previous week. The index reached 6,114.63 on Friday, 14 February 2025, just several points shy of its all-time record high of 6,118.71 from 23 January 2025. The latest update of the alternative futures chart tracks the trajectory of the S&P 500 changing with along with the changing investment horizon for investors.

Here are the week's market-moving headlines, in which the business news media missed the late breaking change in investor expectations for how the Federal Reserve will be setting the Federal Funds Rate in 2025 and beyond.

Monday, 10 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Trump raises tariffs on aluminum, steel imports in latest trade war salvo
      • Where does the US get its steel and aluminum from?
      • US copper price premium soars to record after Trump tariff moves
      • US steel makers stocks rise, foreign peers fall on Trump tariff plans
      • Looming tariffs turned into a mad dash north for one US manufacturer
    • Oil gains nearly 2% despite trade war concerns
  • Fed officials expected to sit on hands for months before next interest rate move:
    • Fed to wait for months on next rate cut as tariffs risk inflation flare up: Reuters poll
  • Bigger trouble developing in China:
    • China to roll back clean power subsidies after boom
    • China's consumer inflation at 5-month high, producer deflation persists
  • Nasdaq, S&P, and Dow ended in the green as investors await inflation data
    • Gold bulls lock in on glittering milestone; $3,000/oz in sight

Tuesday, 11 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil prices climb to 2-week high on supply worries, US tariffs check gains
      • EIA raises US oil production forecast for 2025
  • Fed officials say US economy is okie dokie and so are their monetary policies, so there's no need to rush to cut interest rates faster:
    • Fed's Powell, quizzed about trade, Musk, and bank safety, says economy is fine
      • Powell says there's no rush to ease more given strong economy
    • Fed's Williams says monetary policy is in right place to achieve Fed goals
  • Bigger trouble, stimulus developing in China:
    • Shares of Chinese steelmakers slide as Trump's fresh tariffs add uncertainty on exports
    • China's car sales post biggest drop in almost a year in January
  • Eurozone officials thinking about responding to US steel, aluminum tariffs:
    • EU readies response as Trump hikes steel and aluminium tariffs
      • Germany's trade surplus with US reaches new record
      • German exports rise, industrial production falls
  • Wall Street ends mixed on tariff drama, Fed comments; focus shifts to consumer inflation
    • Wall Street ends mixed as Coca-Cola gains and Tesla drops

Wednesday, 12 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • US consumer inflation increases at fastest pace in nearly 1-1/2 years in January
      • Inflation heats up more than expected in January CPI
      • Hotter January inflation jolts markets, dimming rate cut hopes
      • Few mourn the US penny as Trump moves to kill it
    • Oil prices retreat after report of U.S. crude stockpile rise
    • US fourth-quarter 2024 earnings growth set to be highest in three years
  • Fed officials surprised by inflation, thinking about stalling more rate cuts:
    • Fed's Powell sees a ways to go on shrinking Fed holdings
    • US inflation jump puts Fed officials on notice as Powell testifies
      • Fed's Bostic: Not comfortable cutting rates again until there is more clarity on inflation, other policies
    • Hot Jan US CPI surprise argues for Fed staying in neutral
  • BOJ officials say they're going to aim at 2% inflation for Japan's economy:
    • BOJ to conduct monetary policy to achieve 2% inflation target, Governor Ueda says
    • BOJ governor says higher food prices in Japan may alter inflation expectations
  • ECB officials say they're excited to keep cutting Eurozone interest rates, have dark outlook for Eurozone economy:
    • ECB can keep cutting rates even if Fed takes it slow, Vujcic says
      • Reuters interview with ECB policymaker Vujcic
    • ECB's Nagel wary of blurry 'neutral' rate target, U.S. tariff damage
    • ECB's Villeroy reaffirms that Trump policies will likely be negative for economy
  • S&P 500 ends down as hot US inflation data hints at fewer rate cuts
    • Dividend attention grows as Wall Street digests higher inflation report

Thursday, 13 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • US producer inflation trends higher; labor market remains stable
    • Trump outlines reciprocal tariff plan in latest bid to reshape trade on his terms
      • Trump's memo outlining reciprocal tariffs plans
    • US power companies increase data center demand spending as DeepSeek fears wane
  • Higher inflation pushing back timing of Fed officials' expected 2025 rate cut:
    • Fed seen likely policy hold until September; Traders add bets on July cut
  • BOJ officials expected to keep delivering interest rate hikes because of inflation in Japan:
    • Japan's wholesale inflation jumps, reinforcing BOJ rate-hike bets
    • Most economists expect BOJ to hike rates in latter half of 2025, survey shows
